resources.string: Point camera at ingredient list — works for any product
Informational only — not an official halal certification. When in doubt, contact the manufacturer.
The app needs camera access to scan.

This app checks products for halal status in two ways:

• Barcode scan fetches product info from OpenFoodFacts
• OCR scan reads the ingredient list directly from packaging — works for any product

Ingredients are matched against a database of known haram (pork, alcohol, carmine/E120, shellac/E904, L-cysteine/E920, …) and mushbooh (doubtful: gelatin, mono-/diglycerides E471-E479, bone phosphate E542, natural flavors, glycerin E422, …) substances.

IMPORTANT: This is NOT a fatwa. It is based on the widely-held consensus of Islamic dietary law. When in doubt, choose a certified halal product.
Hold ingredient list inside the frame — text must be readable
Product with barcode %1$s not found in the database.\n\nTip: try \"Scan ingredients\" (OCR) and point the camera at the ingredient list on the packaging.
No text detected. Image not sharp enough or text too small.
Hold the phone steady, ensure good light,\nand make sure the text is sharply readable.
Works for any product — just point the camera at the ingredient list.
Pork or pork-derived product — haram per Quran 2:173
Carmine (E120) — colorant from cochineal insects, considered haram by most scholars
L-Cysteine (E920) — often sourced from pig bristles or human hair
E542 (Bone phosphate) — from animal bones, often pork
Mono-/Diglycerides (E471-E479) — may be animal or plant origin
Stearoyl lactylates (E481/E482) — fatty acid source must be verified
Sorbitan esters (E491-E495) — fatty acid source must be verified
E470 (Fatty acid salts) — source must be verified
E631 (Sodium inosinate) — may come from meat or fish
Glycerin (E422) — may be animal fat, plant or synthetic
E445 (Glycerol esters) — source must be verified
Natural flavor — source not specified, may use alcohol or animal carriers
Flavor extract — carrier may be alcohol
Vanilla extract — usually made with ethanol
Rennet — animal or microbial, source must be verified
Whey — problematic if the cheese was made with animal rennet
Lecithin (E322) — usually soy (halal) but egg-lecithin is possible
Beef gelatin — only halal if from halal-slaughtered cattle
Beef tallow — only halal if from halal slaughter
Chicken/poultry fat — only halal if from halal slaughter
Lamb/sheep fat — only halal if from halal slaughter
E1100 (Amylase) — may be animal or microbial
Natural vanillin — often made with ethanol
E1520 (Propylene glycol) — often a carrier for alcoholic flavors
Magnesium stearate — fatty acid source must be verified (animal or plant)
No haram or doubtful ingredients detected in the list. This is not an official halal certification — for animal products the source should still be verified.
